name: Council on Foreign Relations Full
description: The Council on Foreign Relations (CFR) is a nonpartisan think tank and membership organization dedicated to U.S. foreign policy and international affairs. Founded in 1921, it publishes the influential journal *Foreign Affairs* and hosts discussions and studies on a wide range of global issues. Conspiracy theories surrounding the CFR posit that it is a powerful, secretive organization that manipulates global events to establish a New World Order or advance a specific agenda, often involving the erosion of national sovereignty and the concentration of power in the hands of a global elite. Proponents suggest the CFR's membership, which includes prominent figures in government, business, academia, and media, is evidence of its influence. They point to CFR publications and policy recommendations that align with globalist agendas as proof of its manipulative intent. Mainstream perspectives view the CFR as a respected and influential, but ultimately transparent, organization that contributes to informed public discourse on foreign policy. Academics and analysts argue that the CFR's influence is often overstated and that its policy recommendations are subject to debate and scrutiny within the broader policy-making process. They emphasize the diversity of opinions within the CFR and the lack of evidence supporting claims of a unified, conspiratorial agenda. Criticisms of the conspiracy theories highlight the logical fallacies, selective use of evidence, and lack of verifiable proof used by proponents. Furthermore, some critics argue that these theories often rely on anti-Semitic tropes and xenophobic sentiments.
subtitle: Elite Think Tank or Shadow Government?

claims.claim: CFR secretly controls US foreign policy.
claims.evidence: High percentage of government officials are CFR members; CFR publications influence policy.
claims.criticism: Correlation doesn't equal causation; policy decisions are complex and involve many actors.

claims.claim: CFR aims to establish a New World Order.
claims.evidence: CFR advocates for international cooperation and global governance.
claims.criticism: International cooperation is not inherently conspiratorial; many problems require global solutions.

claims.evidence: CFR supports international agreements and institutions that limit national autonomy.
claims.criticism: International agreements can benefit national interests; sovereignty is not absolute.

keyFigures.name: Carroll Quigley
keyFigures.role: Historian whose work is cited by some conspiracy theorists

keyFigures.name: David Rockefeller
keyFigures.role: Longtime CFR chairman and frequent target of conspiracy theories

keyFigures.name: Richard Haass
keyFigures.role: Current CFR President; author on foreign policy

keyFigures.name: Daniel Drezner
keyFigures.role: Academic who critiques CFR conspiracy theories

mythologyConnections: The idea of a hidden hand guiding events
Allusions to secret societies with hidden knowledge

timeline.event: Conspiracy theories about the CFR emerge and proliferate
CFR continues to operate and influence foreign policy discussions

sources.author: Shoup, Laurence H.
sources.work: Ruling the World: The Hidden History of the Council on Foreign Relations
sources.title: Shoup, Laurence H.

sources.author: Domhoff, G. William
sources.work: Who Rules America? The Corporate Community and the Upper Class
sources.title: Domhoff, G. William

sources.author: Drezner, Daniel W.
sources.work: All Politics is Global: Explaining International Regulatory Regimes
sources.title: Drezner, Daniel W.

worshipPractices.name: Strategic Foresight Rituals
worshipPractices.description: Devotees engage in elaborate scenario planning exercises, attempting to predict future global events and their impact. These sessions often involve complex models and simulations, seen as a way to appease the unpredictable forces of geopolitics.

worshipPractices.name: The Foreign Affairs Liturgy
worshipPractices.description: Regular study and recitation of articles from *Foreign Affairs* are considered essential for understanding the divine will of international relations. Its pages are seen as a sacred text, offering insights into the hidden workings of global power.

festivals.name: The Annual Symposium of Influence
festivals.description: A grand gathering of the initiated, where discussions and debates shape the course of international affairs. Offerings of policy papers and strategic insights are presented to the council elders, in hopes of securing their favor.

festivals.name: The Publication of Foreign Affairs Day
festivals.description: The release of each new edition of *Foreign Affairs* is celebrated as a day of enlightenment. Disciples pore over its contents, seeking guidance and inspiration for their own contributions to the global order.

cultural.modernLegacy: The CFR's pervasive influence can be seen in countless political dramas and thrillers, often portrayed as a shadowy cabal pulling the strings of global events. Its image as an elite think tank has become a shorthand for power and influence in modern popular culture, both admired and feared.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Asgard, home to the Aesir gods, served as a center for strategic planning and decision-making concerning the realms, similar to how the CFR focuses on U.S. foreign policy impacting the world.
The Olympian Gods convened to discuss and decide on matters affecting mortals and the balance of the cosmos, mirroring the CFR's function of analyzing and shaping international relations.

The Ennead, a group of nine powerful deities, represented the collective wisdom and authority in Egyptian cosmology, much like the CFR gathers experts to deliberate on global issues and propose solutions.

Devaloka, the realm of the gods, is where divine beings deliberate on cosmic order and intervene in earthly affairs, similar to how the CFR analyzes and influences U.S. foreign policy and international relations.

associations.name: The Loom of Fate
associations.significance: Like the Fates weaving destiny, the CFR shapes global events through policy influence.

associations.name: Hydra
associations.significance: Addressing one foreign policy challenge (a 'head') can create multiple new ones, mirroring the CFR's complex and multifaceted engagement.

associations.name: Janus
associations.significance: Reflecting the CFR's dual focus on historical analysis and future policy recommendations, Janus represents looking to both the past and the future.

associations.name: Seven
associations.significance: Seven, often associated with wisdom and completeness in mythology, reflects the CFR's aspiration to comprehensively address global issues.

associations.name: Quicksilver
associations.significance: Like quicksilver, the CFR's influence is pervasive and difficult to grasp, shifting and adapting to global dynamics.

associations.name: The Oracle of Delphi
associations.significance: The CFR, like the Oracle, is perceived by some as offering influential (though potentially ambiguous) guidance on critical matters.

associations.name: World Tree
associations.significance: The CFR aims to connect and understand the interconnectedness of global affairs, similar to the World Tree's role in linking different realms.

companions.name: Argus Panoptes
companions.description: Argus, with his hundred eyes, symbolizes constant vigilance and comprehensive oversight, reflecting the CFR's aim to monitor global affairs from multiple perspectives. His watchfulness ensures that threats are detected and addressed promptly.

companions.name: Janus
companions.description: Janus, the Roman god of beginnings, transitions, and doorways, represents the CFR's role in navigating complex geopolitical shifts and forging new paths in foreign policy. His two faces symbolize the consideration of both past lessons and future possibilities.

companions.name: The Oracle of Delphi
companions.description: The Oracle provides insight and foresight on complex issues, similar to the CFR's role in analyzing global trends and offering policy recommendations. Her pronouncements, though sometimes cryptic, guide decision-making.

companions.name: Hermes
companions.description: Hermes, the messenger of the gods, facilitates communication and diplomacy, mirroring the CFR's role in fostering dialogue between nations and promoting international understanding. His speed and adaptability reflect the need for quick responses to global events.

extendedContent.title: The Olympian Council: A Parallel in Power and Influence
extendedContent.content:
In Greek mythology, the Olympian Council, composed of the twelve major deities residing on Mount Olympus, serves as a potent parallel to the perceived influence of the Council on Foreign Relations. Zeus, as the head of the Olympian Council, held ultimate authority, mirroring the alleged control the CFR exerts over global affairs. Decisions made by the Olympians dictated the fate of mortals and even influenced the actions of minor deities, mirroring the CFR's purported influence on international policy and national governments. Hesiod's *Theogony* details the power struggles and alliances within the Olympian pantheon, reflecting the complex dynamics and competing interests that might exist within an organization like the CFR. The Olympian Council, while immortal and divine, still faced internal disagreements and challenges to its authority, suggesting that even powerful entities are not immune to internal strife and external pressures.

Furthermore, the Olympians were often portrayed as acting in their own self-interest, pursuing personal agendas that could have both positive and negative consequences for humanity. This mirrors the criticism leveled against the CFR, which is often accused of promoting policies that benefit specific corporate or national interests at the expense of others. The myths surrounding the Trojan War, for instance, illustrate how the personal rivalries and biases of the Olympian gods directly impacted human events. The parallels are stark, suggesting that the human desire for power, influence, and control, whether embodied in mythical deities or contemporary institutions, remains a constant throughout history.

Ultimately, the Olympian Council represents a powerful, centralized body that wields significant influence over the lives of mortals, a concept that resonates with the anxieties surrounding the perceived power and influence of organizations like the CFR. Just as the ancient Greeks attributed world events to the actions of the gods, modern conspiracy theories often attribute global events to the machinations of powerful, secretive organizations.

extendedContent.title: The Round Table of Camelot: A Quest for Global Order
extendedContent.content:
The Arthurian legend, particularly the Round Table of Camelot, offers another compelling mythological analogue to the Council on Foreign Relations. King Arthur's Round Table was conceived as a symbol of equality and unity, bringing together knights from different backgrounds and regions to serve a common purpose: the establishment of a just and ordered kingdom. This mirrors the CFR's stated goal of promoting international cooperation and stability. However, the ideals of the Round Table were often challenged by internal conflicts, betrayals, and the pursuit of individual glory, demonstrating the difficulty of achieving true unity and consensus, even among those with shared goals.

Malory's *Le Morte d'Arthur* chronicles the rise and fall of Camelot, highlighting the constant tension between the utopian ideals of the Round Table and the realities of human ambition and fallibility. The quest for the Holy Grail, a central theme in Arthurian legend, can be interpreted as a metaphor for the pursuit of a perfect world order, a goal that is both inspiring and ultimately unattainable. The disintegration of the Round Table, brought about by internal strife and moral failings, serves as a cautionary tale about the fragility of even the most well-intentioned organizations.

The CFR, like the Round Table, is often portrayed as a gathering of influential individuals striving to shape the world according to a particular vision. However, the Arthurian legend reminds us that even the noblest intentions can be corrupted by personal ambition, ideological differences, and the inherent complexities of human nature. The quest for global order, like the quest for the Holy Grail, is a never-ending journey fraught with challenges and potential pitfalls.

extendedContent.title: The Norns: Weaving the Tapestry of Global Destiny
extendedContent.content:
In Norse mythology, the Norns are female beings who control the destiny of gods and men. They weave the threads of fate, determining the course of events and shaping the future. Urd (fate), Verdandi (becoming), and Skuld (necessity) represent the past, present, and future, respectively, and their actions are said to be immutable. This concept mirrors the conspiracy theories surrounding the CFR, which often portray the organization as secretly controlling global events and manipulating the future according to its own agenda. The Norns, though powerful, are not necessarily benevolent; their decisions are often inscrutable and can lead to both prosperity and destruction.

The *Poetic Edda* and *Prose Edda* describe the Norns as living at the base of Yggdrasil, the world tree, where they carve runes into its trunk to determine the fate of all beings. This imagery reinforces the idea of a hidden power operating behind the scenes, shaping the world according to a predetermined plan. The concept of a predetermined fate, as dictated by the Norns, aligns with the belief that the CFR is working towards a specific global order, regardless of the consequences for individual nations or populations.

The fear associated with the Norns stems from the perceived lack of control over one's own destiny. Similarly, the fear surrounding the CFR arises from the belief that a small group of individuals is dictating global policy without the consent or knowledge of the general public. Whether it's the Norns weaving the threads of fate or the CFR allegedly shaping global events, the underlying anxiety remains the same: the fear of losing control over one's own life and future.

extendedContent.title: The Shadow Council of the Underworld: Hidden Agendas and Secret Rulers
extendedContent.content:
Across numerous mythologies, the underworld is often governed by a shadowy council or group of figures who operate in secrecy, influencing events in the mortal realm. In Egyptian mythology, the Duat, the realm of the dead, is governed by Osiris and a panel of judges who weigh the hearts of the deceased to determine their fate. This council operates according to strict rules and rituals, but its decisions have profound consequences for the afterlife of every individual. Similarly, in Greek mythology, Hades rules the underworld with the assistance of various deities and figures who carry out his will. These entities are often depicted as being aloof and detached from the concerns of mortals, focusing instead on maintaining order and balance in the realm of the dead.

The idea of a hidden council operating in the shadows, influencing events without the knowledge or consent of the general public, resonates with the conspiracy theories surrounding the Council on Foreign Relations. Just as the rulers of the underworld are often portrayed as having their own hidden agendas and motivations, the CFR is often accused of pursuing its own interests at the expense of others. The secrecy and lack of transparency surrounding the organization only serve to fuel these suspicions.

Furthermore, the underworld is often depicted as a place of hidden knowledge and power. Those who possess access to this knowledge are able to manipulate events in the mortal realm to their advantage. This concept aligns with the belief that the CFR possesses privileged information and expertise that allows it to influence global policy and shape the future according to its own vision. The parallels between the shadowy councils of the underworld and the perceived influence of the CFR highlight the enduring human fascination with secret power and the fear of hidden agendas.

extendedContent.title: The Guardians of Sacred Knowledge: Protecting or Hoarding Global Power?
extendedContent.content:
In many cultures, myths exist about guardians who protect sacred knowledge or powerful artifacts. These guardians, often depicted as dragons, sphinxes, or ancient orders, are entrusted with safeguarding secrets that, if revealed or misused, could have devastating consequences. The Library of Alexandria, though historical, takes on a mythic quality in its representation of a repository of all human knowledge, protected (and ultimately lost) to the ravages of time and human conflict. These guardians are often seen as benevolent, acting to protect humanity from its own destructive tendencies. However, they can also be viewed with suspicion, accused of hoarding knowledge and power for their own benefit, denying access to those who could use it for good.

The CFR, with its access to influential figures, policy expertise, and classified information, can be seen as a modern-day embodiment of these guardians. Proponents argue that the organization serves as a vital resource for policymakers, providing informed analysis and promoting constructive dialogue on critical global issues. They see the CFR as a responsible steward of knowledge, using its expertise to advance U.S. foreign policy and promote international cooperation. However, critics argue that the CFR's privileged access to information and its close ties to government and corporate interests give it undue influence over global affairs. They accuse the organization of hoarding power and using its expertise to promote policies that benefit a select few at the expense of the many.

The debate over the CFR's role mirrors the age-old tension between the need to protect sacred knowledge and the desire to share it for the benefit of all. Whether the CFR is a benevolent guardian or a self-serving hoarder depends largely on one's perspective and beliefs. However, the underlying anxiety remains the same: the fear that power and knowledge, when concentrated in the hands of a select few, can be easily abused.
